Five Nights at Freddy's

The Night Shift

Mike Schmidt needed a job, and Freddy Fazbear's Pizza was hiring. His introduction to the position came through an audio recording left by the previous night guard, a man named Ralph. The message opened warmly enough — a welcome, a rundown of the restaurant, a description of the animatronic mascots that performed for children during the day. Freddy Fazbear, Bonnie the Bunny, Chica the Chicken, and Foxy the Pirate. Ralph explained that the characters used to roam freely around the building but that had changed after an incident in 1987, one he referred to simply as the Bite of '87, adding almost as an afterthought that it was remarkable how much of a normal life a person could live without their frontal lobe. The animatronics had been restricted ever since. During the day they sat on stage. At night, the rules were different. At night, they moved.

What the Walls Were Saying

Ralph suggested Mike make use of the two doors on either side of his office and the camera system covering the building, promised they would talk again tomorrow, and the recording ended. Mike was on his own. Over the course of the week his nights settled into a grim routine of watching and waiting. Eleven cameras covered the building and he cycled through them constantly, tracking four animatronics that had no business being as unsettling as they were. Bonnie and Foxy came from the left. Chica and Freddy from the right. Each had their own rhythm. Bonnie drifted through utility closets and party rooms. Chica wandered into the kitchen. Foxy stayed behind the curtain of Pirate Cove unless Mike neglected to check on him, in which case he came sprinting down the left hallway without warning. Freddy was the most patient, barely moving in the early nights, only becoming dangerous later in the week when he would quietly overtake the others and make his own approach.

The doors and cameras kept Mike alive. The power did not always cooperate. The animatronics were not the only things in the building with Mike. Strange things began happening that he had no framework to explain. The words "It's Me" appeared without warning, whispered through the halls, scrawled across the walls, bleeding through posters and flickering across his camera tablet when the feed glitched. On the cameras, sometimes, there was a fifth figure. A golden bear standing in rooms it had no reason to be in, watching. When Mike looked away and looked back, it was gone. Occasionally it appeared in the office itself, slumped in the corner like something with nothing left holding it together, before vanishing just as quietly as it had arrived. The posters on the walls told their own story.

The cheerful Fazbear advertisements flickered and replaced themselves with newspaper clippings when Mike wasn't looking. Five children had gone missing from the pizzeria on June 26th, 1985. Their bodies were never found. A man in one of the mascot suits had lured them one by one into a back room and no one had ever been charged. In the weeks that followed, customers noticed a smell coming from the animatronics. Health inspectors were called, then the police. The suits appeared to be leaking fluid around the eyes. The children were believed to still be inside them.

The restaurant fought to stay open through the allegations and the rumours but by the end of 1985 the closure was announced. The children remained, officially, missing.


On Mike's final night, Ralph's last recording played. The power had gone out on Ralph's last shift, the doors had stopped responding, and the darkness had let everything in. All that could be heard was the tinny sound of Freddy's music playing somewhere very close, and then a scream, and then nothing. Mike survived his week. His reward was a pink slip informing him that his employment had been terminated for tampering with the animatronics, general unprofessionalism, and an odor.

He was fired. The building was closing anyway. And somewhere inside the suits, five children were still waiting to be found.

Five Nights at Freddy's 2

The New and Improved Freddy Fazbear's Pizza

The grand reopening was announced with all the enthusiasm Fazbear Entertainment could muster. A newspaper celebrated the occasion, promising a vintage pizzeria given new life, a fresh start, a better and safer experience for families everywhere. The night shift paid a hundred dollars and fifty cents a week.

Jeremy Fitzgerald was the man who took the job. A familiar voice greeted him through the phone on his first night. Ralph, running through another set of training recordings, welcomed Jeremy to his new summer job at the new and improved Freddy Fazbear's Pizza. He asked Jeremy to set aside whatever negative impressions he might have carried over from the older location, which Ralph described plainly as having been left to rot. Fazbear Entertainment, he insisted, was committed to family fun and above all else, safety.

The new animatronics were the centrepiece of that promise. Same names, new faces. Freddy, Bonnie, Chica, and Foxy had been rebuilt from the ground up with advanced mobility, facial recognition software, and a direct connection to a criminal database. They were designed to identify threats and keep the building secure. The older models still existed somewhere in the building, slightly more worn, slightly more aggressive, kept around as the company weighed its options for the future. The layout of the new location had changed as well. There were no doors on the office. In their place, Jeremy was given a flashlight. He was also given a Freddy Fazbear head to wear when the animatronics got too close, the idea being that their systems would register him as another mascot and move on.

For the Marionette, the prize counter animatronic that apparently operated on entirely different instincts from the rest, there was a music box. It needed to be wound regularly. If it ran down, whatever the Marionette was holding back would stop being held back. Ralph mentioned, almost casually, that the nightguard before Jeremy had been moved to the day shift after complaining that the animatronics were moving during his shifts, entering his office, and behaving aggressively toward adults. This was presented as a solved problem. Jeremy was not entirely convinced.

The Last Birthday Party

Halfway through Jeremy's week, Ralph's tone shifted. There was an investigation underway involving the restaurant. Fazbear Entertainment was denying any wrongdoing. Ralph advised Jeremy not to make eye contact with the newer animatronics, suggesting someone had tampered with their facial recognition systems. By the final night, the building was going into lockdown. No one in or out. Former employees especially were not to be admitted. Ralph reassured Jeremy that he would be moved to the morning shift once things settled down, and that they were already looking for someone to fill his old position on nights. There was one last event before the closure. A birthday party.

Jeremy survived his week and received his pay on November 12th, 1987. A new night guard named Fritz Smith took over the shift. And on Jeremy's first morning as the day shift guard, while the birthday party was still going, Foxy attacked him. The animatronic bit Jeremy's frontal lobe clean off. It was the Bite of '87, the very incident Ralph had referenced in the original Freddy Fazbear's Pizza recordings as though it were already ancient history.

Fritz lasted one night. Ralph called to inform him that the building was shut down, and that someone had taken a spare yellow suit and done something with it, something that had set the animatronics off entirely. Fritz was fired the following morning. His pink slip cited tampering with the animatronics and his odor. Fazbear Entertainment released a statement acknowledging that the new location had been open for only a matter of weeks before the decision was made to close. The new animatronics would be scrapped for parts. The older models would be kept. The company promised it would return, albeit with a smaller budget. The doors closed. The lights went out. And whatever had been done with that yellow suit stayed behind in the dark with everything else.

Five Nights at Freddy's 4

The House on the Hill
The Afton household sat alone on a hill, away from everything. It was a broken home in the most literal sense — a father who had long since crossed lines that couldn't be uncrossed, an older son carrying guilt he hadn't yet learned to name, and a youngest boy who was afraid of almost everything, but especially of his father's creations.

David Afton was the youngest in the house and the most vulnerable. He had a deep and genuine terror of the animatronics, the kind that lived in his chest and wouldn't leave no matter how many times people told him they were just machines. His older brother Michael had little sympathy for it. To Michael, David's fear was a toy to be played with. He locked David in his room. He jumped out at him wearing the head of David's own Foxy plush. He dragged him to Fredbear's Family Diner and locked him inside the utility and maintenance closets, knowing full well what the sight of those golden suits did to his little brother.

Somewhere in the house there had once been a girl's room. A broken Funtime Foxy toy still sat inside it. The room had belonged to another Afton, though she was gone now, and nobody spoke about her. A countdown had begun for David's birthday. In the nights leading up to it, a small Fredbear plush sat with David and spoke to him, encouraging him, telling him not to be scared. It was the only voice in the house that seemed to be on his side.

The Bedroom

Michael's nights in that house were not restful. The years he had spent working inside Fazbear's buildings had left marks on him, and those marks had followed him home and into the dark. At night he found himself back on the job, guarding a bedroom door the same way he had once guarded an office. Nightmare versions of the animatronics came for him from the closets and the hallways, Freddy, Bonnie, Chica, and Foxy twisted into something larger and more wrong than they had ever been in waking life. Fredbear appeared too, pressing in from the edges of a house that felt smaller every night. He checked the doors. He flashed the closets. He watched the bed. And sometimes, in the middle of it all, he would see other things. Pills. An IV bag. Flowers left beside a bed. The last things his little brother had seen before he died. Michael's mind was not only replaying his own fear. It was replaying David's final days, over and over, in the only place he couldn't escape them. Two days before the birthday, David had been found crying on the floor of Fredbear's Family Diner. The Fredbear plush urged him to remember what he had seen and to leave, but David collapsed the moment an employee in the Fredbear suit approached him. On his way home he ran into children waiting for his upcoming party, some of them taunting him for being a crybaby, whispering that the animatronics came alive at night and stuffed children into the suits, never to be found again. David arrived home with all of that sitting on top of everything else he was already carrying.

The Bite of '83

On David's birthday, Michael decided to make it memorable. He gathered his friends, each of them wearing a Fazbear facemask, and together they grabbed David and carried him toward the Fredbear animatronic on stage. The plan was simple enough. They were going to give him a birthday kiss from Fredbear. A prank. A laugh. The kind of thing that would be a story later. What none of them had accounted for was that Fredbear was a springlock suit, and David was terrified and shaking and crying, and the springlocks reacted to the tears and the movement and the chaos exactly the way springlocks always did when moisture was involved. Fredbear's mouth snapped shut. David was rushed to urgent care. He lay in a hospital bed surrounded by pills and flowers and the soft hum of machines, barely there, fading slowly. Michael came to him and did the only thing left available to him. He apologised. He spoke to his brother not knowing whether David could hear a single word, and he said he was sorry for all of it. Then another voice reached David, quieter and stranger than his brother's. It came through the Fredbear plush sitting nearby. It told David that he was broken. It told him it could fix him. It promised that it was still his friend, and that it would put him back together. David slipped away not long after. The bite of '83 became the moment that split the Afton family in two and set everything that followed into motion. At the time, it was meant to be the end of the story. It was anything but.
